Taxonomic Identification Reference

Genus-Level Identification of Cryptic Crustacean Decapods from Apo Reef Natural Park

Location: Apo Reef Natural Park, Sablayan, Occidental Mindoro, Philippines
Method: Autonomous Reef Monitoring Structures (ARMS) deployed 2017, retrieved October 2024
Stations: Station 10 (sheltered) & Station 4 (exposed), Apo Menor
Specimens: 138 decapod specimens (80 crabs, 58 shrimps) preserved in 95% ethanol
Biogeographic context: Coral Triangle, Indo-West Pacific — shallow reef cryptofauna

Summary of Genus Assignments

Comparing original tracking-sheet assignments against revised identifications. Flagged rows indicate recommended changes.

Family Original Genus Revised Genus Confidence Status
Portunidae Thalamita Thalamita Moderate Confirmed — check Thranita split
Pilumnidae Palimnus Pilumnus Moderate–High Corrected (typo in original)
Trapeziidae Trapezia Trapezia High Confirmed
Xanthidae Etisus Leptodius Low–Moderate Revised — size & morphology inconsistent with Etisus
Porcellanidae Petrolisthes Petrolisthes High Confirmed
Percnidae Percnon Percnon High Confirmed
Galatheidae Galathea Galathea Moderate–High Confirmed
Alpheidae Alpheus Alpheus High Confirmed
Palaemonidae Palaemon Periclimenes s.l. Low–Moderate Revised — reef habitat inconsistent with Palaemon

Detailed Identifications

Portunidae Moderate
Thalamita

Original assignment confirmed. Note: several former Thalamita species have been transferred to Thranita gen. nov. (Evans, 2018). Verify whether your species falls under the revised Thalamita s.s. or Thranita.

Diagnostic Features

Carapace transversely hexagonal, wider than long, with a broad frontal margin bearing 4–6 low lobate teeth. Anterolateral margins with approximately 5 sharp teeth/spines. Chelipeds robust but not massively enlarged. Fifth pereiopods clearly paddle-shaped (swimming legs). Specimen small (~15–20 mm CW), consistent with cryptic reef portunids. Dorsal surface relatively smooth with faint transverse ridges. Well-developed orbits with prominent eyes visible in frontal view.

Reasoning

The small size, hexagonal carapace with 5 anterolateral teeth, broad multilobed front, and reef-crevice habitat strongly point to Thalamita — the most speciose and commonly encountered portunid genus in Indo-West Pacific coral reef cryptofauna. Portunus tends to be larger with a more elongate last anterolateral spine. Charybdis typically has 6 anterolateral teeth and a differently shaped front. Thalamita is one of the dominant portunid genera recovered from ARMS in the Coral Triangle. Rock, rubble, and reef flat habitats are the primary ecological niche for this genus in the Indo-West Pacific.

Microhabitat Preference

Thalamita species are primarily rock, rubble, and reef flat dwellers. They inhabit crevices among coral rubble, under loose rock on reef flats, and within the interstices of dead coral frameworks. They are particularly abundant in shallow subtidal reef habitats (0–15 m), often in areas with moderate water flow. In ARMS, they colonize both the open and closed plate interspaces, favoring dark crevice-like microhabitats that mimic their natural reef rubble refugia.

Caveats

Frontal lobe count and exact anterolateral tooth number difficult to confirm at this image resolution. Species-level ID requires frontal teeth shape, cheliped carpal spines, and male gonopod morphology. The Thalamita/Thranita/Zygita/Trierarchus split (Evans, 2018) should be checked — Thalamita crenata and T. sima remain in Thalamita s.s., but T. prymna and T. spinimana are now Thranita.

Key References
  • Ng, P.K.L., Guinot, D. & Davie, P.J.F. (2008). Systema Brachyurorum. Raffles Bulletin of Zoology, Suppl. 17, 1–286.
  • Evans, N. (2018). Molecular phylogenetics of swimming crabs (Portunoidea) supports a revised family-level classification. Mol. Phylogenet. Evol., 127, 904–918.
  • Apel, M. & Spiridonov, V.A. (1998). Taxonomy and zoogeography of portunid crabs of the Arabian Gulf. Fauna of Arabia, 17, 159–331.
Pilumnidae Moderate–High
Pilumnus

Corrected from "Palimnus" — original tracking sheet likely contains a typographical error. The correct genus is Pilumnus Leach, 1815.

Diagnostic Features

Carapace ovate to subhexagonal, roughly as wide as long, moderately convex. Dorsal surface covered with distinct spinules/tubercles and dense long setae — the classic "hairy crab" appearance over the entire body. Anterolateral margins with approximately 3–4 distinct spines or teeth. Chelipeds robust and unequal, with granulate/spinulose surfaces. Walking legs heavily setose. Front bilobed and moderately wide.

Reasoning

Pilumnus is the type genus of Pilumnidae and the most commonly encountered genus of this family on Indo-West Pacific coral reefs. The combination of a setose body, spinulose carapace, unequal chelipeds with granulate surfaces, and 3–4 anterolateral teeth is diagnostic. Heteropilumnus has fewer, more prominent spines with less dense setae. Actumnus has a smoother, more granulate surface with shorter setae. Cryptopilumnus is very similar but requires microscopic examination to distinguish.

Microhabitat Preference

Pilumnus species are ubiquitous inhabitants of coral rubble, rocky reef crevices, and algal turf in intertidal to shallow subtidal habitats. They shelter within the complex three-dimensional structure of dead coral heads, under loose rocks, among sponge and ascidian fouling communities, and inside ARMS plate interspaces. Their dense setae trap sediment and detritus, providing camouflage. They are generalist omnivores/scavengers and among the most commonly recovered brachyuran genera from reef cryptofauna surveys worldwide.

Caveats

Species-level ID requires close examination of anterolateral tooth shape, cheliped dactylus dentition, and male gonopod morphology. Dense setae can obscure underlying carapace sculpture. Some pilumnid genera (e.g., Cryptopilumnus) are very similar and may require microscopic examination.

Key References
  • Ng, P.K.L., Guinot, D. & Davie, P.J.F. (2008). Systema Brachyurorum. Raffles Bulletin of Zoology, Suppl. 17, 1–286.
  • Ng, P.K.L. & Clark, P.F. (2014). The genus Pilumnus in the western Indian Ocean. Zootaxa, 3893(1), 1–64.
  • Davie, P.J.F. (2002). Zoological Catalogue of Australia, Vol. 19.3B. CSIRO Publishing.
Trapeziidae High
Trapezia

Original assignment confirmed with high confidence.

Diagnostic Features

Carapace distinctly trapezoidal/squarish, wider than long, smooth to gently convex dorsal surface with faint transverse ridges. Frontal margin broad. Anterolateral margins short and smooth (lacking prominent teeth). Chelipeds moderate-sized and subequal. Walking legs somewhat flattened, adapted for gripping coral branches. Lower specimen is an ovigerous female carrying a conspicuous egg mass.

Reasoning

Trapeziidae contains only two extant genera: Trapezia and Tetralia. The smooth, broad carapace with no anterolateral teeth, moderate subequal chelipeds, and trapezoidal outline are characteristic of Trapezia. Tetralia differs by having a proportionally wider and more rectangular carapace, smaller overall body size, and distinctly different cheliped morphology (thinner, more elongate fingers). ARMS from coral reef substrate makes Trapezia highly expected — they are obligate symbionts of branching corals.

Microhabitat Preference

Trapezia are obligate symbionts of living branching corals, particularly Pocillopora and Acropora. They live as mated pairs within the interbranch spaces of their host colony, rarely leaving. They actively defend their host coral against predators such as the crown-of-thorns starfish (Acanthaster planci) and against sediment smothering by fanning with their walking legs. This mutualistic relationship is one of the best-documented examples of crustacean–cnidarian symbiosis on coral reefs. Their recovery from ARMS likely reflects colonization from adjacent coral colonies or fragments incorporated into the ARMS structure.

Caveats

Species-level ID requires examination of live color patterns (largely lost in ethanol), cheliped dentition, and walking leg dactylus morphology. Castro (1997) is the key monograph for species-level resolution. The retained brownish coloration may be informative but is altered by preservation.

Key References
  • Castro, P. (1997). Trapeziid crabs of the Indo-West Pacific region. Crustaceana Monographs, 1, 1–220.
  • Castro, P. (1999). Trapeziidae of Indonesia. Zoologische Mededelingen, 73, 27–61.
  • Ng, P.K.L., Guinot, D. & Davie, P.J.F. (2008). Systema Brachyurorum. Raffles Bulletin of Zoology, Suppl. 17, 1–286.
Xanthidae Low–Moderate
Leptodius

Revised from Etisus — specimen size (~10–15 mm CW) and smooth carapace morphology are inconsistent with Etisus, which is typically much larger (CW >30 mm) with coarser ornamentation. Leptodius or Xanthias are stronger candidates.

Diagnostic Features

Carapace broadly oval, convex, wider than long, smooth to finely granulate surface. Shallow grooves define carapace regions (protogastric, hepatic, branchial). Frontal margin broad and bilobed. Anterolateral margins with approximately 4 low, blunt teeth. Chelipeds robust and unequal with dark-tipped (black) fingers — the classic xanthid hallmark. Walking legs moderately robust. Orange-red coloration retained in preservation.

Reasoning

Etisus species tend to be considerably larger with a more coarsely granulate to tuberculate carapace and more pronounced anterolateral teeth. The small size, smooth carapace with well-defined shallow regional grooves, blunt anterolateral teeth, and black-fingered chelipeds are more consistent with Leptodius, which has 12 well-recognized species in the Indo-West Pacific, or Xanthias (which tends to have a slightly more granulate surface). In the Philippines (eastern Indian Ocean to central Pacific), the relevant species is likely Leptodius affinis (De Haan, 1835) rather than L. exaratus, which is restricted to the western Indian Ocean. Leptodius species are ubiquitous components of the intertidal and shallow subtidal rocky/coral reef fauna across the Indo-West Pacific.

Microhabitat Preference

Leptodius species are characteristic inhabitants of the rocky intertidal and shallow subtidal zones. They shelter under and inside pieces of coral rubble, within rock crevices, and among algal assemblages. They are among the most abundant xanthid crabs in reef flat and rubble zone habitats, frequently found in association with coralline algae-encrusted rubble. Their small size and cryptic coloration (often matching the rubble substrate) make them classic cryptofauna. They are omnivorous, feeding on algae, detritus, and small invertebrates.

Caveats

Xanthidae sensu lato has been extensively reorganized; assignment to Xanthidae sensu stricto should be verified. Species-level ID requires cheliped dactylus dentition, male gonopod structure (first gonopod shape is the key character separating L. affinis from L. exaratus), and fine carapace sculpture. The retained reddish-orange color is altered by ethanol. Molecular barcoding is strongly recommended given the taxonomic complexity of this group. Cannot fully exclude juvenile Etisus without size measurements and gonopod examination.

Key References
  • Serène, R. (1984). Crustacés Décapodes Brachyoures de l'Océan Indien occidental et de la Mer Rouge. Xanthoidea. Faune Tropicale, 24, 1–400.
  • Lee, S.K., Mendoza, J.C.E., Ng, P.K.L. & Kim, W. (2013). On the identity of Leptodius exaratus. Raffles Bulletin of Zoology, 61(1), 189–204.
  • Ng, P.K.L., Guinot, D. & Davie, P.J.F. (2008). Systema Brachyurorum. Raffles Bulletin of Zoology, Suppl. 17, 1–286.
Porcellanidae High
Petrolisthes

Original assignment confirmed with high confidence.

Diagnostic Features

Carapace rounded to broadly oval, strongly flattened dorsoventrally, wider than long. Dorsal surface smooth to finely granular. Frontal margin broad and deflexed. Chelipeds conspicuously flattened and broad — the hallmark of Petrolisthes. Only three functional pairs of walking legs visible (fifth is reduced and tucked). Lateral view clearly shows the strongly flattened body plan. Chelipeds subequal and proportionally large relative to body size.

Reasoning

Petrolisthes is by far the most speciose and commonly encountered porcellanid genus on Indo-West Pacific coral reefs, with over 100 species in the region. The combination of smooth, flattened carapace, broad flattened chelipeds, and three visible walking leg pairs is diagnostic. Pachycheles has more robust, inflated chelipeds. Pisidia is smaller with a more circular carapace. Neopetrolisthes has a more distinctly ornamented carapace and is specifically associated with sea anemones.

Microhabitat Preference

Petrolisthes species are extremely common on rocky shores and coral reefs, living under rocks, within coral rubble, and in reef crevices from the intertidal to shallow subtidal zones. They are filter feeders, using their modified maxillipeds (with feathery setae) to sweep plankton and suspended organic particles from the water column. Their strongly flattened body allows them to inhabit very narrow spaces between rocks and coral plates — a morphology ideally suited for ARMS plate interspaces. They are often found in aggregations. Some species are associated with sponges or other invertebrates, while most are free-living rock and rubble dwellers.

Caveats

Species-level ID requires examination of the cheliped carpus ornamentation (crest or spines on anterior margin), walking leg dactylus shape, and male gonopod morphology. Some species groups within Petrolisthes may be separable at genus level pending ongoing revisions.

Key References
  • Osawa, M. & McLaughlin, P.A. (2010). Annotated checklist of anomuran decapod crustaceans: Porcellanidae. Raffles Bulletin of Zoology, Suppl. 23, 109–129.
  • Osawa, M. (2007). Porcellanidae from New Caledonia. Zootaxa, 1548, 1–49.
  • Werding, B. & Hiller, A. (2007). Porcellanidae of the Red Sea. Zootaxa, 1460, 1–24.
Percnidae High
Percnon

Original assignment confirmed. Percnon is the only commonly encountered genus within Percnidae in the Indo-West Pacific.

Diagnostic Features

Carapace transversely ovate to hexagonal, wider than long, moderately flattened. Walking legs conspicuously long and slender relative to body size — the most immediately striking and diagnostic feature. Chelipeds small and subequal, much smaller than the walking legs. Eyes prominent on well-developed orbits. Anterolateral margins with at least one distinct tooth/spine. Dorsal surface smooth to finely granular. Lateral view shows extremely flattened body profile. Bottom specimen appears to be an ovigerous female with an egg mass.

Reasoning

Percnidae is a small family (formerly within Plagusiidae or Grapsidae), and Percnon is the only genus commonly encountered in the Indo-West Pacific. The extremely long, slender walking legs, small chelipeds, and flattened body profile are unmistakable. There is essentially no alternative genus within the family in this biogeographic region.

Microhabitat Preference

Percnon species are agile, extremely flat-bodied crabs that inhabit wave-exposed rocky shores and coral reef surfaces, particularly in the intertidal and very shallow subtidal zones. They are herbivorous/omnivorous, grazing on algae on exposed rock and coral surfaces. Their flattened body allows them to press tightly against the substrate to avoid dislodgment by wave action, and to squeeze into very narrow crevices. They are typically found on reef flats, surge channels, and wave-exposed reef margins — fast-moving and difficult to capture. Their presence in ARMS is notable, as they likely colonize the external surfaces and open plate interspaces. Percnon planissimum is the most widespread Indo-West Pacific species and a known reef algae grazer.

Caveats

Species-level ID requires frontal margin shape, anterolateral tooth count, walking leg dactylus morphology, and male gonopod structure. P. planissimum is the most widespread species, but P. abbreviatum and P. affine also occur. Color pattern useful for species discrimination but likely faded in ethanol.

Key References
  • Ng, P.K.L., Guinot, D. & Davie, P.J.F. (2008). Systema Brachyurorum. Raffles Bulletin of Zoology, Suppl. 17, 1–286.
  • Schubart, C.D. (2011). Phylogenetic relationships within Grapsidae and trans-isthmian distributions. Zootaxa, 2920, 1–29.
  • Crosnier, A. (1965). Crustacés Décapodes: Grapsidae et Ocypodidae. Faune de Madagascar, 18, 1–143.
Galatheidae Moderate–High
Galathea

Original assignment confirmed.

Diagnostic Features

Body elongate and flattened dorsoventrally — classic squat lobster morphology. Carapace with a well-developed, pointed rostrum projecting anteriorly. Dorsal surface shows distinct transverse ridges (striae). Chelipeds long, slender, and spinose, held extended forward. Walking legs well-developed. Abdomen flexed beneath thorax but retains a functional tail fan. Specimens small (~5–8 mm CL), pale from ethanol preservation.

Reasoning

Galathea is the most speciose genus in Galatheidae and the most commonly recovered galatheid from ARMS and coral reef cryptofauna in the Indo-West Pacific. The transverse carapace striae, spinose rostrum, and slender spinose chelipeds are characteristic. Phylladiorhynchus has a distinctly more flattened, leaf-shaped rostrum. Coralliogalathea tends to be specifically associated with antipatharian or gorgonian corals. The family has undergone significant revision with the establishment of Munididae as separate from Galatheidae sensu stricto.

Microhabitat Preference

Galathea species inhabit reef crevices, coral rubble, and the undersides of rocks from intertidal to deep-water habitats. They are suspension feeders and scavengers, using their long chelipeds to pick food particles from the water and substrate. On coral reefs, they are found in the interstices of dead coral frameworks, under living coral colonies, within sponge cavities, and inside ARMS plate interspaces. Some species are commensal with other invertebrates such as crinoids, sponges, and gorgonians. Their small size and elongate body shape allow them to exploit narrow crevice habitats. They are among the most frequently recovered anomuran crustaceans from Coral Triangle reef cryptofauna.

Caveats

The family Galatheidae has undergone significant revision. Identification beyond genus level requires rostral spine count, carapace stria number and ornamentation, cheliped spine patterns, and male gonopod morphology. Molecular confirmation would be particularly valuable given the species diversity in the Coral Triangle.

Key References
  • Baba, K., Macpherson, E., Poore, G.C.B., et al. (2008). Catalogue of squat lobsters of the world. Zootaxa, 1905, 1–220.
  • Macpherson, E. & Robainas-Barcia, A. (2015). Galathea of the Indian and Pacific Oceans. Zootaxa, 3947(1), 1–168.
  • Poore, G.C.B. (2004). Marine Decapod Crustacea of Southern Australia. CSIRO Publishing.
Alpheidae High
Alpheus

Original assignment confirmed. Specimen shown is the top shrimp in the combined plate.

Diagnostic Features

Body compact and robust for a caridean shrimp. One chela distinctly enlarged relative to the other — the defining feature of snapping shrimps. Rostrum short. Eyes partially covered by the carapace (orbital hoods), characteristic of Alpheus. Overall body shape stocky rather than slender. Pale/whitish from ethanol preservation.

Reasoning

Alpheus is overwhelmingly the most speciose genus in Alpheidae, with over 300 species worldwide and hundreds in the Indo-West Pacific. The combination of compact body, one distinctly enlarged snapping chela, short rostrum, and eyes covered by orbital hoods is diagnostic. The main alternative is Synalpheus, which differs in having fully covered eyes, a shorter and stouter body, and often a more pronounced rostrum. The visible orbital hoods (partially but not fully covered eyes) and body proportions favor Alpheus.

Microhabitat Preference

Alpheus snapping shrimps are the most abundant and diverse macrocrustacean group in coral reef cryptofauna. They inhabit burrows in sand and rubble, crevices within coral frameworks, sponge interiors, and the interstices of dead coral heads. Many species live in mated pairs in self-constructed burrows, while others are commensal with corals, sponges, or sea anemones. Some species (the A. djiboutensis complex) are famous for their mutualistic association with gobies. Their snapping claw produces a cavitation bubble used for territorial defense, prey stunning, and intraspecific communication. In ARMS, they are among the most frequently recovered taxa, colonizing the dark, enclosed plate interspaces that mimic their natural crevice habitats.

Caveats

Species-level identification requires detailed examination of the major chela morphology (notch/groove presence on dactylus), minor chela form, walking leg dactylus shape, and rostral carina. Many cryptic species complexes exist within Alpheus. Molecular barcoding is strongly recommended for species-level work.

Key References
  • Chace, F.A. Jr. (1988). The caridean shrimps of the Albatross Philippine Expedition, Part 5: Alpheidae. Smithsonian Contributions to Zoology, 466, 1–99.
  • Banner, D.M. & Banner, A.H. (1982). The alpheid shrimp of Australia. Part III. Records of the Australian Museum, 34, 1–357.
  • De Grave, S. & Fransen, C.H.J.M. (2011). Carideorum catalogus. Zoologische Mededelingen, 85, 195–589.
Palaemonidae Low–Moderate
Periclimenes sensu lato

Revised from Palaemon — Palaemon is predominantly estuarine/coastal and unlikely from coral reef ARMS. The slender body, well-developed toothed rostrum, and reef habitat context point to a coral-associated pontoniine palaemonid, most likely Periclimenes sensu lato. Note: this genus is polyphyletic and currently being split into numerous genera (Ancylomenes, Actinimenes, Cristimenes, Rapimenes, Cuapetes, Laomenes, Hamodactylus, etc.).

Diagnostic Features

Body slender and laterally compressed — typical palaemonid habitus. Rostrum well-developed with dorsal and ventral teeth, extending beyond the eyes. Antennae long. Second pereiopods appear to bear chelae. Overall body translucent/pale from preservation. Specimen small.

Reasoning

Palaemon sensu stricto is predominantly estuarine and coastal, and is not commonly recovered from coral reef ARMS. The reef habitat context strongly favors a pontoniine palaemonid. Periclimenes s.l. is the largest "wastebasket" genus of symbiotic pontoniine shrimps, with species associated with corals, anemones, echinoderms, sponges, and other invertebrates on Indo-West Pacific reefs. However, the genus is polyphyletic and actively being revised — many former Periclimenes have been transferred to Ancylomenes, Cuapetes, Laomenes, Actinimenes, Cristimenes, and Rapimenes (Ďuriš & Horká, 2017; Okuno & Bruce, 2010). Without host organism data or microscopic examination, assignment to a specific genus within the pontoniines is not feasible from this image alone.

Microhabitat Preference

Periclimenes s.l. species are predominantly symbiotic shrimps associated with a wide range of marine invertebrate hosts on coral reefs: sea anemones, scleractinian corals (including mushroom corals), soft corals, echinoderms (sea stars, crinoids, sea urchins), sponges, and ascidians. The specific microhabitat depends entirely on the host — some live within the tentacles of anemones, others within the interbranch spaces of corals, and still others on the oral disc of fungiid corals. Some species are cleaner shrimps, using their host as a station for cleaning reef fish. In ARMS, pontoniine shrimps likely colonize from adjacent host organisms or exploit the ARMS structure as temporary shelter.

Caveats

This is the most uncertain identification. The single lateral view severely limits what can be assessed. Confident genus-level ID in coral-associated palaemonids requires the mandible (palp presence/absence), maxilliped morphology, second pereiopod finger dentition, and knowledge of the associated host organism. Molecular barcoding is essentially required for reliable identification. The genus Periclimenes is actively being dismantled — use "Periclimenes s.l." as a placeholder and expect it to change as taxonomy stabilizes.

Key References
  • De Grave, S. & Fransen, C.H.J.M. (2011). Carideorum catalogus. Zoologische Mededelingen, 85, 195–589.
  • Ďuriš, Z. & Horká, I. (2017). Towards a revision of Periclimenes: resurrection of Ancylocaris and designation of three new genera. ZooKeys, 646, 25–44.
  • Okuno, J. & Bruce, A.J. (2010). Designation of Ancylomenes gen. nov. Zootaxa, 2372, 85–105.
  • Horká, I., De Grave, S., Fransen, C.H.J.M., Petrusek, A. & Ďuriš, Z. (2016). Multiple host switching events shape the evolution of symbiotic palaemonid shrimps. Scientific Reports, 6, 26486.

Cryptic Crustacean Decapod Assemblage in Apo Reef Natural Park, Sablayan, Occidental Mindoro
BS Biology (Zoology) — University of the Philippines Los Baños
Genus-level identifications based on macroscopic morphology of ethanol-preserved specimens. All identifications are provisional and should be verified with microscopic examination and molecular barcoding where possible.
